content.text: Why Simple Reflex Agents Are Not Enough
Simple reflex agents require that the current percept contains
everything

needed to choose the right action. In real environments this assumption breaks constantly: sensors fail, rooms are around a corner, doors are behind you. A model-based reflex agent solves this by maintaining an
internal state
— a belief about what the world looks like — and firing its rules against that belief rather than the raw percept.

The agent still uses condition-action rules (making it a reflex agent), but the conditions are evaluated against the agent's model of the world, not a single snapshot from its sensors. This makes the agent effective in partially observable environments.

attrs.alt: Model-based reflex agent: percept updates internal world model; condition-action rules fire on belief state, not raw percept
attrs.title: Model-Based Reflex Agent: Internal State Loop

content.text: The Three Components of a World Model
A world model has three parts working together on every step:
Component
What it does
Example (vacuum cleaner)
Internal state
The agent's current belief about the world
{location: A, A_dirty: True, B_dirty: True}
Transition model
Predicts how actions change the world
clean → current room no longer dirty
Sensor model
Maps world state to expected percepts
dirt sensor reads True when room is dirty
Belief state update
Combines transition + sensor to maintain belief
update() called on every act() cycle

Each time the agent perceives the world, it first applies the transition model to predict what its last action should have changed, then overwrites those predictions with the actual sensor reading. Sensor data takes priority over prediction; predictions fill gaps when sensors fail.
Implementing a Model-Based Reflex Agent
The class below separates the update logic (world model) from the decision logic (rules), which makes each independently testable:
Example: Vacuum Cleaner with Room Memory

A simple reflex vacuum cleaner has to sense dirt in a room before it cleans. A model-based vacuum remembers which rooms it has already cleaned, so it does not revisit clean rooms just because the sensor temporarily fails:
The key line is
self.state[f"{loc}_dirty"] = False
in the transition model. When the agent executes a
clean

action, the model records that the current room should now be clean — even before the next sensor reading confirms it. This allows the agent to correctly decide to move to the other room, even if the dirt sensor is not checked again immediately.
Handling Sensor Failure

One of the most important use cases for a model-based agent is graceful degradation when sensors are unavailable. The traffic agent below uses its transition model to predict the expected light colour from the cycle timing, falling back to the safe 'stop' action only if the sensor has been missing too long:

# Traffic agent that survives sensor gaps
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class Rule:
    condition: Callable[[dict], bool]
    action: str

class TrafficWorldModel:
    SAFE_SEQUENCE = ["red", "red-amber", "green", "amber"]

    def __init__(self):
        self.state = {"light": "red", "ticks_since_update": 0}
        self._phase_idx = 0
        self._ticks_per_phase = {0: 30, 1: 3, 2: 25, 3: 5}  # seconds
        self._ticks = 0

    def update(self, percept: dict, last_action: str | None) -> None:
        self._ticks += 1
        if percept.get("light") is not None:
            # Sensor reading available: trust it, re-sync phase
            self.state["light"] = percept["light"]
            self.state["ticks_since_update"] = 0
        else:
            # Sensor failed: use transition model to predict next phase
            self.state["ticks_since_update"] += 1
            ticks_in_phase = self._ticks % sum(self._ticks_per_phase.values())
            cumulative = 0
            for phase, duration in self._ticks_per_phase.items():
                cumulative += duration
                if ticks_in_phase < cumulative:
                    self.state["light"] = self.SAFE_SEQUENCE[phase]
                    break

traffic_model = TrafficWorldModel()
traffic_rules = [
    Rule(condition=lambda s: s["ticks_since_update"] > 10, action="stop"),  # sensor failure
    Rule(condition=lambda s: s["light"] in ("red", "red-amber"), action="stop"),
    Rule(condition=lambda s: s["light"] == "amber", action="prepare-to-stop"),
    Rule(condition=lambda s: s["light"] == "green", action="go"),
]
A simple reflex agent would have no rule that matches a
None
percept and would default to the wrong action (or crash). The model-based agent uses its internal prediction to keep acting correctly through short sensor outages.
Belief States with Uncertainty

In some domains the agent cannot know the true world state even after seeing a percept — sensors are noisy. In these cases the internal state becomes a probability distribution over possible world states (a belief state), updated using Bayes' rule:

# Belief state update with uncertainty (probabilistic model)
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class ProbabilisticModel:
    """
    Tracks probability distribution over possible world states.
    Simple example: p(door_open) as a float in [0, 1].
    """
    p_door_open: float = 0.5  # prior
    # Sensor model parameters
    p_sensor_true_given_open: float = 0.9    # sensor says open when open
    p_sensor_true_given_closed: float = 0.1  # sensor says open when closed

    def observe(self, sensor_reading: bool) -> None:
        """Bayesian update: P(open | reading) via Bayes rule."""
        if sensor_reading:
            likelihood_open   = self.p_sensor_true_given_open
            likelihood_closed = self.p_sensor_true_given_closed
        else:
            likelihood_open   = 1 - self.p_sensor_true_given_open
            likelihood_closed = 1 - self.p_sensor_true_given_closed
        p_open   = likelihood_open   * self.p_door_open
        p_closed = likelihood_closed * (1 - self.p_door_open)
        normaliser = p_open + p_closed
        self.p_door_open = p_open / normaliser

    def most_likely_state(self) -> str:
        return "open" if self.p_door_open >= 0.5 else "closed"

model = ProbabilisticModel(p_door_open=0.5)

# Noisy sensor reads "open" three times in a row
for _ in range(3):
    model.observe(True)
print(f"P(open) = {model.p_door_open:.3f}")  # ~0.986
print(model.most_likely_state())              # open

# One contradictory reading
model.observe(False)
print(f"P(open) = {model.p_door_open:.3f}")  # ~0.854 (still probably open)
print(model.most_likely_state())              # open

This Bayesian update is the foundation of more sophisticated approaches like particle filters and Kalman filters. For most practical agentic AI tasks, however, a deterministic model (trusted sensor overrides prediction) is sufficient.
Testing a Model-Based Agent
Because the model and the rules are separate, testing strategy splits accordingly:
Test the world model in isolation.
Verify that the internal state is correctly updated by each action and each percept. These are pure unit tests.
Test the rules against crafted belief states.
Construct belief state dicts directly (bypassing the sensor) and verify the rules fire the expected actions.
Integration test the full agent.
Run the agent over a sequence of percepts and check the action sequence.

# Testing a model-based agent requires testing the state update
# logic separately from the rule-matching logic.
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class Rule:
    condition: Callable[[dict], bool]
    action: str

class VacuumWorldModel:
    def __init__(self):
        self.state = {"location": "A", "A_dirty": True, "B_dirty": True}
    def update(self, percept, last_action):
        if last_action == "move_right": self.state["location"] = "B"
        elif last_action == "move_left": self.state["location"] = "A"
        elif last_action == "clean": self.state[self.state["location"] + "_dirty"] = False
        if percept.get("dirt") is not None:
            self.state[self.state["location"] + "_dirty"] = percept["dirt"]

# --- Unit test the world model in isolation ---
def test_clean_action_updates_model():
    m = VacuumWorldModel()
    assert m.state["A_dirty"] is True
    m.update({}, last_action="clean")
    assert m.state["A_dirty"] is False  # model reflects the cleaning

def test_percept_overrides_prediction():
    m = VacuumWorldModel()
    m.update({}, last_action="clean")   # model predicts A is now clean
    m.update({"dirt": True}, last_action=None)  # sensor contradicts
    assert m.state["A_dirty"] is True   # sensor wins

def test_sensor_gap_preserves_belief():
    m = VacuumWorldModel()
    m.update({}, last_action="clean")   # model: A clean
    m.update({"dirt": None}, last_action=None)  # no sensor data
    assert m.state["A_dirty"] is False  # belief held from prior update

test_clean_action_updates_model()
test_percept_overrides_prediction()
test_sensor_gap_preserves_belief()
print("All model tests passed")
When to Use a Model-Based Reflex Agent
Upgrade from a simple reflex agent to a model-based agent when:
The environment is partially observable.
Sensors can fail, have limited range, or return noisy data.
History matters for correct decisions.
The right action at step N depends on something that happened at step N-2 or earlier.
Actions have delayed effects.
An action changes the world, but the sensor does not confirm the change until the next cycle.
You need graceful sensor failure handling.
The agent should continue to operate correctly (or safely) when a percept is missing.
If the task additionally requires planning ahead (choosing a sequence of actions to reach a goal), a simple rule set will not suffice and you should consider a goal-based or utility-based agent.
Comparing Simple Reflex and Model-Based in Practice
The difference between the two agent types becomes concrete when you trace through the same sequence of events:
Scenario
Simple reflex
Model-based reflex
Sensor reads current value
Acts on the reading
Updates belief, then acts
Sensor returns None
No rule matches → default
Uses prior belief + transition model
Action takes two cycles to complete
Cannot represent in-progress state
Stores action-in-progress in state
Two conflicting sensor readings
Takes the last rule that matches
Sensor model resolves conflict via probabilities
Managing Belief Staleness
A model-based agent's internal state can become
stale

— it reflects the world as it was, not as it is. This is especially relevant in slow-updating environments where sensors are polled infrequently. The solution is to attach a timestamp and expiry to each belief, and treat expired beliefs as unknown:
Staleness-aware models prevent the agent from acting on outdated beliefs. When a belief expires, the agent either requests a fresh reading or falls back to a safe default action.
LLM-Backed World Models

Hard-coding transition and sensor models requires domain expertise and becomes difficult when the world state has many interacting variables. An alternative is to use an LLM as the belief state updater, letting it reason about how new observations modify the existing belief:

# Model-based reflex agent using an LLM to maintain belief state
# Useful when the world model is too complex to hard-code
import json
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

class LLMWorldModel:
    def __init__(self):
        self.state: dict = {}
        self._history: list[dict] = []

    def update(self, percept: dict, last_action: str | None) -> None:
        """Use LLM to merge new observation into existing belief state."""
        self._history.append({"action": last_action, "percept": percept})
        prompt = (
            f"Current belief state: {json.dumps(self.state)}\n"
            f"Last action: {last_action}\n"
            f"New observation: {json.dumps(percept)}\n"
            "Update the belief state JSON to reflect this new information. "
            "Return only valid JSON, no other text."
        )
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
        )
        try:
            self.state = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            pass  # keep previous state on parse failure

# When to use this pattern:
# - world state has many interacting variables
# - the transition model is too complex to encode as if/else
# - flexibility matters more than cost/latency
# When NOT to use:
# - high-frequency updates (LLM too slow and expensive)
# - safety-critical systems (non-deterministic updates)
This pattern trades
determinism and speed
for
flexibility

. Use it when the complexity of the world model is the main obstacle and the call rate is low enough to afford LLM latency and cost. For high-frequency control loops, always prefer a hard-coded transition model.
Summary
A model-based reflex agent adds an internal state to a simple reflex agent, allowing it to act correctly in partially observable environments.
The world model has two parts: a transition model (how actions change the world) and a sensor model (how the world produces percepts).
Rules are evaluated against the belief state, not the raw percept — so the agent handles missing or contradictory sensor data gracefully.
The model and the rules are separate concerns and should be tested independently.
When a task requires lookahead or multi-step planning rather than reactive rules, move to a goal-based or utility-based agent.
